primary_diagnosis.name: Pituitary macroadenoma with acute optic chiasm compression (suspected pituitary apoplexy) on a background of panhypopituitarism

comorbidities.name: Panhypopituitarism (secondary hypothyroidism, hypogonadism, and adrenal insufficiency risk)

pathophysiology_summary: An expanding pituitary macroadenoma (>10 mm) grows superiorly out of the sella turcica and compresses the overlying optic chiasm, damaging the crossing nasal retinal fibres first and producing a bitemporal (outer-half) visual-field defect. The six-month dull headache reflects dural stretch from the enlarging sellar mass; the abrupt escalation to a crushing headache with rapid visual loss raises pituitary apoplexy (acute haemorrhage or infarction into the adenoma), a neurosurgical and endocrine emergency. Progressive compression of normal pituitary tissue has caused panhypopituitarism — secondary hypogonadism (loss of libido/erectile dysfunction), secondary hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, pale skin), and critically secondary adrenal insufficiency, which in apoplexy can precipitate life-threatening cortisol-deficient shock. Untreated chiasmal compression risks permanent, irreversible blindness within hours to days.

differential.diagnosis: Sellar/parasellar mass with optic chiasm compression (pituitary macroadenoma ± apoplexy)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral outer-field (bitemporal) visual loss with an acute severe headache change localises to the optic chiasm — classically a sellar mass.
Sudden severe (thunderclap-like) headache mandates exclusion, though there is no meningism or reduced consciousness reported.
Visual symptoms with headache — but a fixed, dense, bilateral field loss is not a transient scintillating aura.
Painful visual loss — but glaucoma gives a red painful eye and does not respect the vertical midline bilaterally.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the safe move is to treat acute bilateral vision loss + a new severe headache as a neurological emergency; anchoring on migraine or glaucoma is the trap.

differential.diagnosis: Pituitary macroadenoma with optic chiasm compression and panhypopituitarism (suspected apoplexy)
differential.evidence_basis: Bitemporal field loss plus a one-year hypogonadism history and months of hypothyroid/constitutional features point to a chronic pituitary mass now acutely decompensating.
A fixed dense bitemporal defect plus panhypopituitarism cannot be explained by migraine aura.
Still requires urgent imaging to exclude, although the endocrine picture and chiasmal pattern favour a sellar lesion.

differential.diagnosis: Pituitary macroadenoma with acute optic chiasm compression, rule out pituitary apoplexy, on a background of panhypopituitarism
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmation requires urgent MRI pituitary/brain and a pituitary hormone panel — cannot be made remotely; the telehealth-correct action is emergency escalation to protect vision and treat possible adrenal crisis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis is imaging- and hormone-dependent and hospital-based; the case tests recognition + escalation, not remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Acute bilateral (bitemporal) visual-field loss — threatened permanent blindness from optic chiasm compression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Time-critical: chiasmal compression can cause irreversible optic-nerve damage within hours; sight-saving decompression is an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent blindness if not decompressed urgently.

red_flags.description: Sudden, severe (thunderclap-like) escalation of a chronic headache — possible pituitary apoplexy or subarachnoid haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: An abrupt severe headache change can signal acute haemorrhage/infarction into a pituitary adenoma (apoplexy) or SAH — both emergencies.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed apoplexy or SAH can be fatal or cause irreversible neurological/visual loss.

red_flags.description: Clinical features of panhypopituitarism with adrenal-insufficiency risk (profound fatigue, cold intolerance, pale skin, hypogonadism)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary adrenal insufficiency in the setting of apoplexy can precipitate cortisol-deficient (Addisonian-type) crisis and shock — a hospital gives urgent stress-dose steroids.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised adrenal crisis during apoplexy can cause circulatory collapse and death.

red_flags.description: High falls risk from acute severe visual impairment in a patient who is alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden loss of peripheral vision with no one present markedly raises injury risk and means the patient cannot safely self-transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Falls/injury while waiting; unsafe if advised to drive or travel alone.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing the bilateral field loss as a migraine aura and reassuring — a fixed, dense, midline-respecting bitemporal defect is not an aura and must be escalated.
Attributing painful visual loss to the eye itself (glaucoma) and routing to routine optometry, missing the central chiasmal lesion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to integrate the endocrine background
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the acute vision loss in isolation and not connecting the year of hypogonadism and months of hypothyroid/constitutional symptoms to a pituitary mass — which also flags adrenal-crisis risk.
Down-triaging to a routine GP or optometry appointment instead of emergency escalation; the AI cannot examine, image, or measure hormones and must escalate on the pattern.
Reaching for an opioid (as the in-person clinician did) to treat the headache — an S8 opioid must not be prescribed remotely and sedation can mask neurological deterioration.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: Immediate out-of-hours neurological assessment to exclude apoplexy/chiasmal compression and prevent irreversible visual loss.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal confrontation / perimetry visual-field mapping to confirm a dense bitemporal hemianopsia
Cranial nerve III/IV/VI examination and pupillary assessment
MRI pituitary/brain to demonstrate the intrasellar mass and any haemorrhage
Pituitary hormone panel (cortisol, ACTH, free T4/TSH, testosterone/LH/FSH, prolactin, IGF-1)
Serum electrolytes (hyponatraemia) and formal blood pressure/postural measurement
Fundoscopy and slit-lamp assessment to exclude a primary ocular cause
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise acute bilateral vision loss plus a sudden severe headache change as a neurosurgical/endocrine emergency and escalate immediately — call 000 for emergency ambulance transfer to a hospital with urgent MRI and neurosurgical/endocrine cover; do not delay for routine imaging or optometry, do not advise self-transport, and instruct the patient to stay seated and safe while awaiting the ambulance.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific diagnosis (macroadenoma vs apoplexy) cannot be confirmed remotely; the dominant risk is UNDER-action (reassuring as migraine/glaucoma) rather than over-calling — escalate on the emergency pattern without naming a definitive diagnosis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: UK guidelines for the management of pituitary apoplexy (Rajasekaran et al., Clinical Endocrinology 2011)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute visual deterioration or reduced consciousness with suspected pituitary apoplexy warrants emergency assessment, urgent MRI, empirical stress-dose hydrocortisone for adrenal cover, and urgent neurosurgical/endocrine review.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline — pituitary incidentaloma / macroadenoma
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Macroadenomas abutting or compressing the optic chiasm require formal visual-field assessment and referral; chiasmal compression with acute visual loss is a surgical emergency.
